my_sentence: Today was a holiday, so I can with my family go some places to play.
correction: Today was a holiday, so I was able to go to some places with my family.
explanation: Don't split the verb phrase with 'with my family'. Keep 'go to some places' together and put 'with my family' after it. Use 'was able to' for a past ability/opportunity.
correct_examples: I was able to visit several places with my family.
We went to the park with our kids.

explanation: Don't use 'some' to soften an adjective like 'different'. Either say 'looks different' or 'looks a bit/a little different'.
correct_examples: Your room looks different today.
It looks a little different from before.
context: The teacher noticing Jack's hotel background

my_sentence: One of my coworkers who always later to the company.
correction: One of my coworkers who was always late.
explanation: 'Later' is the comparative form; for the simple quality use the base adjective 'late'. The relative clause also needs a verb: 'who was always late'.
correct_examples: He was always late for work.
The colleague who was often late got a warning.
context: Practising relative clauses with a business example

my_sentence: One of my coworkers is going to fly to the Tokyo next month.
correction: One of my coworkers is going to fly to Tokyo next month.
explanation: Don't use 'the' before most city names. There is only one Tokyo, so it takes no article.
correct_examples: She flew to Tokyo last week.
We're traveling to Paris in June.

my_sentence: My manager and I are going to be visit our customer.
correction: My manager and I are going to visit our customer.
explanation: After 'be going to' use the base verb directly: 'are going to visit'. Don't insert 'be' before the main verb. (Adding 'be' would make it passive, where the subject receives the action.)
correct_examples: We are going to visit the client tomorrow.
They are going to launch the product soon.
context: Practising relative clauses with company roles

my_sentence: If someone is ambitious, he or she will have a big dream that need to be achieved.
correction: If someone is ambitious, he or she will have a big dream that he or she needs to achieve.
explanation: Inside the relative clause give it a subject and an agreeing verb: 'that he or she needs to achieve'. 'That need' is missing the subject and the third-person -s.
correct_examples: She has a goal that she wants to achieve.
It's a target that the team needs to reach.
context: Building sentences about an ambitious person

my_sentence: I want to say something there.
correction: I want to see something there.
explanation: You meant 'see' (to observe), but it came out as 'say' (to speak). Keep the vowels distinct: 'see' /iː/ vs 'say' /eɪ/.
correct_examples: I want to see how people live there.
I'd like to see it for myself.

my_sentence: I want to go to a develop country, not a developing country.
correction: I want to go to a developed country.
explanation: Use the adjective 'developed' (past participle) for a rich, advanced country. 'Develop' is the base verb; 'developing' describes a country still growing.
correct_examples: Japan is a developed country.
They moved to a developed nation for better pay.
context: Why Jack wants to travel abroad

my_sentence: How about their life, their situations?
correction: How about their lives and their situations?
explanation: For several people use the plural 'lives' (life → lives). Match the plural already used in 'situations'.
correct_examples: Their lives changed completely.
We discussed their lives and challenges.
context: Curiosity about people's lives in North Korea

explanation: 'Danger' is a noun; the adjective is 'dangerous'. After 'will not' you need 'be' plus the adjective: 'will not be dangerous'.
correct_examples: It won't be dangerous.
The trip will be safe.
context: Reassuring the teacher the trip is safe

my_sentence: Someone who is proactive can succeed easily than others.
correction: Someone who is proactive can succeed more easily than others.
explanation: When comparing with 'than', the adverb 'easily' needs the comparative form 'more easily'. Most two-syllable+ adverbs form the comparative with 'more'.
correct_examples: He learns more easily than his peers.
They finished more quickly than expected.

my_sentence: If someone is punctual, he or she does not want to late.
correction: If someone is punctual, he or she does not want to be late.
explanation: 'Late' is an adjective, so it needs 'be': 'want to be late'. You can't follow 'to' directly with an adjective.
correct_examples: She doesn't want to be late.
I try not to be late for meetings.

my_sentence: If someone is diligent, he or she tried to finish his or her work well.
correction: If someone is diligent, he or she finishes his or her work well.
explanation: For a general, always-true description use the simple present ('finishes'), not the past 'tried'. Match the present tense of 'is' in the first clause.
correct_examples: A diligent person finishes tasks carefully.
She always completes her work on time.

my_sentence: If someone is gentle, he or she can treat others well.
correction: If someone is gentle, he or she treats others well.
explanation: For a definite, characteristic fact, use the simple present without a modal. Only use 'can/will/may' when there's possibility or future meaning, not for a sure statement.
correct_examples: A gentle person treats others kindly.
He speaks softly and respects everyone.
context: The teacher's rule about not overusing modal verbs for sure statements

my_sentence: One of my coworkers who is optimistic can face many challenges at work, and he will not give up.
correction: One of my coworkers who is optimistic can face many challenges at work, and he does not give up easily.
explanation: Use 'will' only for future events. For a general habit or trait, use the simple present 'does not give up'.
correct_examples: He never gives up easily.
She doesn't quit when things get hard.

my_sentence: One of my friends who is a pessimistic likes to stay at home all day.
correction: One of my friends who is pessimistic likes to stay at home all day.
explanation: Don't put 'a' before an adjective. Either use the adjective alone ('is pessimistic') or 'a' + noun ('is a pessimist').
correct_examples: He is pessimistic about the future.
She is a pessimist by nature.

my_sentence: The boy who lived in the same neighbourhood with me is ambitious.
correction: The boy who lived in the same neighbourhood with me was ambitious.
explanation: Keep the tense consistent. Since 'lived' is past, use 'was', not the present 'is'.
correct_examples: The boy who lived next door was very kind.
My old classmate who sat beside me was funny.
context: Describing an ambitious boy from the past

my_sentence: Even though the boy is diligent, couldn't get a high score at school.
correction: Even though the boy is diligent, he couldn't get a high score at school.
explanation: The main clause needs a subject. Add the pronoun 'he' before 'couldn't'.
correct_examples: Although she studied hard, she failed the test.
Even though he tried, he didn't win.
